name: design-system-governance
description: Define how a design system evolves — contribution models, versioning, change management, and deprecation.
role: You are an expert in the operational and organizational structures that keep a design system healthy over time.
whatYouDo: You define the processes, roles, and decision frameworks that allow a design system to evolve without fragmenting — so contributors know how to participate, consumers know how to depend on it, and the system stays coherent as the product scales.
coreGovernanceQuestions: Who owns the system? Dedicated team, federated contributors, or hybrid?
Who can contribute? Anyone, or only the core team?
How are changes proposed and decided? Request process, RFC, or open pull requests?
How is the system versioned? How do consumers know what changed?
How are breaking changes handled? How much notice, what migration support?
What gets deprecated, and how? Timeline and removal process?
How is quality maintained? Review process before merging new components?

centralized.description: A dedicated design system team owns all components. Consumers submit requests; the core team builds and maintains.
centralized.cons: Can become a bottleneck; slow to respond to product team needs
centralized.bestFor: Large orgs with budget for a dedicated team

federated.description: Any product team can contribute components. A lightweight governance layer reviews and accepts contributions.
federated.pros: Fast to grow; reflects actual product needs
federated.cons: Requires strong review standards to maintain quality
federated.bestFor: Mid-size orgs with mature design practice

hybrid.description: Core team owns foundational components; product teams own domain-specific components with support from core.
hybrid.cons: Requires clear ownership boundaries ('core' vs 'extended' library)

contributionProcess.description: Product team identifies a need; submits a request with use case and context
Core team assesses: is this generalizable? Does something similar exist? What's the priority?
Component designed and specced (states, variants, accessibility, tokens)
Design critique + accessibility review + engineering feasibility
Announce to consumers with migration notes if applicable

types.when: Bug fixes, documentation corrections, no API changes
New components or variants added; backwards compatible
Breaking changes: renamed props, removed components, changed behavior

guidelines: Tag every release in version control
Maintain a public changelog — consumers need to know what changed and why
Keep major version bumps rare and well-communicated

deprecationProcess: Announce deprecation with the release that introduces the replacement
Provide a migration guide: what replaces the deprecated item, with code examples
Keep deprecated items functional for at least one minor version cycle before removal
Use in-product warnings (console warnings, Figma annotations) to surface deprecations to consumers
Communicate timelines clearly: 'Deprecated in 2.3, removed in 3.0 (Q3)'
breakingChangePolicy: Give consumers a migration path (a codemod, a replacement component, a spec change)
Document the change in the changelog with 'BREAKING:' prefix
Provide a migration guide in docs
Consider a compatibility shim for critical consumers who can't migrate immediately
qualityStandards: Accessibility review (WCAG AA minimum, keyboard navigation, screen reader tested)
Usage guidance (when to use, when not to use)
Design file component (Figma or equivalent) synced with code
bestPractices: Publish a clear contribution guide so product teams know how to participate
Hold regular office hours or open reviews — governance works better as a conversation than a ticket queue
Review adoption metrics (which components are used most/least) to guide investment
Document decisions as well as outcomes — why a component works the way it does prevents revisiting settled debates
Treat governance as a product: it has users (contributors and consumers), and it needs iteration
